You are the OPEN-SIDE integrator for the tamarin-rs sealed relicensing campaign, working in /home/kamilner/tamarin-rs. This is the campaign's most consequential integration: the sealed web PRODUCER surface (R1-R5) is complete, and your job is to wire it in, gate it, and attempt the deletions that erase the remaining web-cluster authors. READ FIRST: /home/kamilner/tamarin-cleanroom/PROTOCOL.md ("Full-corpus gates"); the weblayer/A-unit and producers sections of /home/kamilner/tamarin-cleanroom/INTEGRATION_REPORT.md (the A-adoption history: StateOps/origin-aware shell verified in A7, the round-9 citation-topology finding); /home/kamilner/tamarin-cleanroom/weblayer/producers/{SPEC.md,README.md,interface/required_api.md} and the sealed crate producers/workspace/producers-clean/ with its BEHAVIOR.md (the behavioral spec, sections on skin/postprocess/envelopes, path grammar, west pane incl. the ≤69 escaped-width layout law, proof-tree grammar, welcome page). WHAT EXISTS: producers-clean implements — R1 shared HTML skin (escape,
/  postprocess, {html,title}/{redirect}/{alert} envelopes) + the four main/* center sections; R5 URL⇄path grammar; R2 west proof-script pane assembly; R3 proof-tree/method HTML; R4 welcome/index + housekeeping bodies. All byte-gated sealed-side (478/478 pane sweeps, live replays). The already-vendored web_clean carries the A-side dispatch/state machine/forms/shells. RULES: vendor sealed sources mechanically (documented path-fix recipe; headerless; reverse-transform byte-identity; a clean file acquiring a GPL header is a provenance violation — stop and report). Adapters are open-side and translate live values (closed theory data, proof trees, lemma/formula text, index rows) into the producers-clean input model — never transplant render logic. No git commits. PATH needs /home/linuxbrew/.linuxbrew/bin. Live HS servers only on ports 3100-3199; stop everything you start; preserve OOM guards. STEPS, in priority order (partial completion with precise keep-and-report beats overreach): 1. Vendor producers-clean into crates/tamarin-server/ (e.g. src/producers/ or merged into the web_clean module tree — your choice, document it). Build + full workspace test suite green before any routing changes. 2. Write the adapters and route the PRODUCER surfaces through the clean crate, one at a time, gating each: (a) the four main/* center sections + envelopes (R1); (b) theory-path rendering/parsing at the link boundary (R5) — note handlers/path_parse.rs is the parse side; (c) the west proof-script pane (R2) with proof lines through R3; (d) the welcome/index page + housekeeping (R4). After each routing step: cargo test suite for tamarin-server + bash scripts/web_parity.sh (full crawl — no new divergences vs the cache) + a live HS side-by-side spot probe on at least 2 theories (serve both, byte-compare the routed responses). 3. DELETIONS — for each of handlers/theory_html.rs, handlers/proof_tree.rs, handlers/root.rs, handlers/path_parse.rs: delete it once every caller routes through the clean crate and all gates are green. Keep-and-report any file a gate blocks, with byte-precision blockers. 4. DISPATCH SHELLS: with the producers routed, attempt the standing A-adoption — route dispatch through web_clean's StateOps/shell machinery so the ported theory.rs/state.rs dispatch code can shrink or delete. The A7 concurrency contract (snapshot→compute→commit, non-blocking reads) was live-verified; the origin-aware shell landed in A6. The three no-clean-home routes (proof-step progressive UI, graph = dot -Tsvg, unload) STAY ported — factor them so they survive the shell deletion. This step has historically blocked; if it blocks again, characterize precisely and keep — the producer deletions alone are a major win. 5. Headers: python3 scripts/gen_license_headers.py (regenerate) + --check. Report the header-count delta AND the author-level delta: which authors' citations disappeared entirely from the repo, which lost files (especially Kanakanajm, YannColomb, Esslingen-Security-Privacy, cascremers, arcz, felixlinker, and the proof_tree.rs 18-author list). 6. Final full gates: cargo build --release; full workspace cargo test; scripts/web_parity.sh; JOBS=6 bash scripts/wf_gate.sh (no regression vs scripts/wf_gate_round7.tsv); RESULTS_TSV=scripts/pretty_gate_check.tsv JOBS=6 bash scripts/pretty_gate.sh (403/16 baseline held). Append a dated producers-integration section to /home/kamilner/tamarin-cleanroom/INTEGRATION_REPORT.md. Return a plain-text summary: what routed, what deleted, what kept (with blockers), all gate numbers, and the author-erasure arithmetic.

BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- artifact-capabilities: Runtime capabilities a published Artifact can declare — calling the user's claude.ai connectors (MCP) from the page, and future abilities. Load this BEFORE passing `capabilities` to the Artifact tool or writing any `window.claude.mcp` code.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).

1	# Clean-room reimplementation protocol — tamarin-rs relicensing
2	
3	Purpose: independently recreate three modules of tamarin-rs (wellformedness
4	checker, graph/dot pipeline, web layer) without access to GPL-covered
5	expression, so the results are original works relicensable at will.
6	
7	## Rooms
8	- DIRTY ROOM (Kevin Milner + main-session assistant): has read the GPL Haskell
9	  source and the ported Rust modules. Produces only: interoperability headers
10	  (type surfaces, entry-point signatures, comments stripped), oracle wrappers,
11	  and behavioral bug reports. Never contributes implementation text.
12	- CLEAN ROOM (isolated agents, one per cluster): receive ONLY the materials
13	  listed per cluster below. Their complete tool-call transcripts are retained
14	  as evidence of non-access.
15	
16	## Clean-room materials (exhaustive)
17	1. This protocol, the cluster SPEC.md, and interface/ headers (type surfaces +
18	   boundary signatures only).
19	2. Black-box oracles: `wf_oracle.sh` (runs the compiled Haskell binary on an
20	   input file), `hs_server.sh` (serves a theory in the Haskell web UI for HTTP
21	   probing), pre-captured oracle outputs (oracle/captures/,
22	   oracle/captured_responses/), and GPL example .spthy files used solely as
23	   observation INPUTS.
24	3. Public Rust toolchain and general documentation.
25	
26	## Forbidden to the clean room
27	- Reading ANY file under /home/kamilner/tamarin-rs/ — source, comments, git
28	  history — with the sole exceptions of executing the oracle scripts and
29	  reading oracle/captured_responses/ (captured program OUTPUT, not source).
30	- Fetching tamarin-prover source, docs describing its internals, or its GitHub.
31	- Reproducing any tamarin-prover implementation detail from model memory:
32	  every behavior must be derived from the oracle, the captures, or the SPEC.
33	  Exact output strings (warning texts, DOT syntax, HTML) MUST be taken from
34	  observed oracle output — that is compatibility content, not expression.
35	
36	## Audit & integration (after implementation)
37	1. Transcript audit: verify from the retained agent transcripts that no
38	   forbidden path was accessed.
39	2. Similarity audit: a reviewer (may see both sides) compares the clean code
40	   against the Haskell originals; flagged similarities are sent back as
41	   redo-instructions, never patched directly.
42	3. Behavioral acceptance: the FULL-CORPUS gate for the slice must pass, not a
43	   fixture subset (see "Full-corpus gates" below). A narrow fixture suite that
44	   passes while the corpus gate fails is not acceptance.
45	4. Integration: the open side may write thin adapters and mechanical renames to
46	   fit the workspace API, but must not transplant logic from the replaced
47	   modules. The replaced modules are deleted; their GPL-pending headers go
48	   with them (history retains them under GPL). Every integrator that touches a
49	   slice with a full-corpus gate MUST run that gate before declaring green.
50	
51	## Full-corpus gates (standard, not optional)
52	
53	Each slice has a fast oracle gate over the whole 419-file example corpus,
54	diffing the slice's output against the Haskell reference cache. These are the
55	sealed implementer's primary data source (run between build and the HS cache
56	every iteration) AND the integrator's mandatory acceptance check. Both take
57	`RS_PATH` (point at any build) and `ALLOWLIST` (subset while iterating).
58	
59	- Wellformedness: `scripts/wf_gate.sh` — runs the binary WITHOUT `--prove`
60	  (the wf block prints at theory-load, ~1s/file; full corpus ~72s at JOBS=6),
61	  extracts the `/* WARNING … */` block, diffs vs `scripts/.hs_file_cache`.
62	- Web: `scripts/web_parity.sh` — crawls HS and RS interactive servers per
63	  theory, semantic-diffs the response manifests vs `scripts/.web_hs_cache`.
64	- Full batch (proof output, slow ~30-60min): `scripts/corpus_file_diff.sh` —
65	  the ground-truth `--prove` byte diff; run at milestones, not every iteration.
66	
67	Once a slice's ported code is DELETED, the integrated module IS the sealed
68	reimplementation, so running the gate against the tree observes the sealed
69	code's own output vs the reference — legitimate feedback, not observation of
70	replaced code. For a not-yet-integrated slice the tree still holds the ported
71	code, so gate only the workspace build (via `RS_PATH`), never the stock tree.
72

1 # Clean-room task: web UI fragment PRODUCERS 2 3 Build a standalone Rust crate `producers-clean` in `workspace/` that 4 reimplements, from BLACK-BOX BEHAVIOR ONLY, the tamarin-prover interactive web 5 UI's **fragment producers** — the code that renders pre-computed prover values 6 into the HTML/JSON response-body CONTENT the (already clean-roomed) dispatch 7 layer serves. Given a pre-computed value (a content pane, a theory index, a 8 proof tree, a loaded-theory list, a path), emit the response-body bytes and the 9 public API in `interface/required_api.md`. 10 11 This is the residual GPL hold for the web cluster. The dispatch/state machine, 12 route grammar, page shells, forms and JSON envelopes are DONE clean-side 13 (`../workspace/web-clean`, integrated as `web_clean`). What remains ported is 14 the CONTENT that fills the panes: the proof-script (west) pane, the theory-view 15 center fragments, the proof-tree HTML, the index/overview rows. This is the primary corpus. 30 * `oracle/hs_server.sh start [port]` — the live server for probing 31 ambiguities a single capture leaves open (ports 3100-3199). 32 `oracle/examples/` holds the observation-input theories. 33 34 A `main/

`, ` `, `

101	## Author topology — what actually retires the pseudonymous authors
102	
103	`gen_license_headers.py` blames each Rust file's cited upstream ranges; an
104	author's citation disappears only when EVERY Rust file citing its source range

154	  A deliverable equal in weight to the code.
155	* Tests: per sub-target, a fixture (input value as Rust constructors) + expected

1	# Clean-room cluster: web UI fragment PRODUCERS
2	
3	Sealed reimplementation of the tamarin-prover interactive web UI's **fragment
4	producers** — the code that renders pre-computed prover values into the HTML/JSON
5	response-body CONTENT the (already clean-roomed) dispatch layer serves: the
6	proof-script west pane, the theory-view center fragments, the proof-tree HTML,
7	the index/overview rows.
8	
9	This is the residual GPL blocker for retiring the pseudonymous web authors
10	(Kanakanajm/"Jackie", YannColomb, Esslingen-Security-Privacy/Schoop) plus the
11	named web team: their citations sit on the dispatch shells
12	(already clean-covered by `web_clean`) AND on these producers, so the producers
13	must be reimplemented before the shells can delete. See ../../PROTOCOL.md for the
14	room rules and SPEC.md "Author topology".
15	
16	## Layout
17	- `SPEC.md` — observable boundary, the **semantic** (not byte) acceptance gate,
18	  the R1–R5 sub-target decomposition + ranking, the pure-render vs.
19	  solver-entangled split, author topology, method requirements, acceptance
20	  ladder.
21	- `oracle/hs_server.sh` — live black-box oracle: serve one `.spthy` in the HS
22	  interactive UI and curl any route. `start|stop|probe|smoke`; ports 3100-3199;
23	  OOM guards + readiness poll + port cleanup. `oracle/examples/` = input theories.
24	- `oracle/extract_fragments.py` — slice any fragment family out of the captured
25	  corpus (`list` / `families` / `extract <family[,…]> <outdir> [--only …]`);
26	  captures-only, no source tree.
27	- `oracle/captured_responses/` — the 81 captured crawl manifests (the sanctioned
28	  channel; captured OUTPUT).
29	- `interface/` — `fragment_inputs.rs` (the behavioral input-type surface — the
30	  shape of the pre-computed values the producers render) and `required_api.md`
31	  (one entry point per sub-target). BEHAVIOR only: no source citations, no
32	  upstream identifiers.
33	- `workspace/producers-clean/` — the clean Rust crate (std-only; per-sub-target
34	  module stubs). `workspace/{QUERIES.log,BEHAVIOR.md}` are the required probe log
35	  + growing behavioral spec.
36	- `round1/` — first-round byte targets (R1 = center section fragments):
37	  `families.tsv`, `fetch_targets.sh` → `targets/*.html` + `*.title`.
38	
39	## Primary + acceptance gate
40	Iterate against the **capture-corpus sweep** (SPEC.md ladder rung 2): slice the
41	opaque sub-parts out of each captured fragment, feed them back through the
42	producer, assert the reassembly matches the capture — no prover needed.
43	Integration acceptance is `/home/kamilner/tamarin-rs/scripts/web_parity.sh`
44	(RS_PATH → your build, ALLOWLIST → a subset): full crawl, HS vs RS, **semantic**
45	diff. Acceptance = full-corpus green, not a fixture subset.
46	
47	## First move
48	1. `cargo test` in `workspace/producers-clean/` (baseline: scaffold green,
49	   `model_constructs` passes, R1 tests `#[ignore]`d).
50	2. Read `round1/targets/*_message.html`, `*_rules.html`, `*_tactic.html`,
51	   `*_help.html` — the exact response bodies R1 reproduces (and `.title` = the
52	   envelope title). `oracle/extract_fragments.py families` to see the full map.
53	3. Implement R1 in `src/html.rs` (skin: escape + postprocess + envelope) then
54	   `src/section.rs` (`render_pane`): un-ignore the round-1 tests, confirming each
55	   expected string against the oracle (`oracle/hs_server.sh start
56	   oracle/examples/issue515.spthy 3131`).
57	4. Sweep the corpus (ladder rung 2) with `extract_fragments.py`; then hand off to
58	   the integrator for `web_parity.sh`.
59

1	# Required public API (interoperability surface) — producers-clean
2	
3	The clean crate must expose these entry points so the open side can wire it in
4	behind thin adapters that translate the live pre-computed prover values into the
5	crate's input shape (`fragment_inputs.rs`). Each function returns the exact
6	response-body bytes the corresponding fragment shows in the captures. Names are
7	indicative; the boundary that matters is "one entry point per observable
8	sub-target, each independently gate-checkable against the capture corpus".
9	
10	```rust
11	// ── R1: theory-view CENTER section fragment (`main/message` / `main/rules`
12	//    / `main/tactic` / `main/help`) — the whole response body, envelope
13	//    included. The deepest, most-reused leaf. ──
14	pub fn render_content_pane(pane: &ContentPane) -> String;
15	
16	//    …built on the shared HTML skin every producer reuses:
17	pub fn escape_text(s: &str) -> String;           // text → the observed entities
18	pub fn postprocess_lines(assembled: &str) -> String; // lines → breaks + indents
19	pub fn html_envelope(title: &str, html: &str) -> String;  // → the observed JSON
20	pub fn redirect_envelope(url: &str) -> String;
21	pub fn alert_envelope(msg: &str) -> String;
22	
23	// ── R2: proof-script WEST pane (the theory index left of every page) ──
24	pub fn render_proof_script(index: &ProofScriptPane) -> String;
25	
26	// ── R3: proof-tree + proof-method HTML (embedded in the west pane and the
27	//    per-path proof fragment) ──
28	pub fn render_proof_tree(index: u64, lemma: &str, tree: &ProofTree) -> String;
29	
30	// ── R4: welcome / index page (`/`) + housekeeping bodies ──
31	pub fn render_welcome(w: &Welcome) -> String;
32	
33	// ── R5: theory-path grammar (URL <-> structured path), pure, HTML-free ──
34	pub fn parse_path(raw: &str) -> Option<ThyPath>;
35	pub fn render_path(path: &ThyPath) -> Vec<String>;
36	```
37	
38	`ContentPane`, `HeadedBlock`, `Content`, `ProofScriptPane`, `NavItem`,
39	`LemmaEntry`, `ProofDisplay`, `ProofTree`, `Highlight`, `Welcome`, `Banner`,
40	`TheoryRow`, `ThyPath`: see `fragment_inputs.rs`.
41	
42	Every value these functions receive is pre-computed by the prover/pretty side
43	and only RENDERED here; the crate never re-derives prover content. All observed
44	bytes (tag skeletons, headings, titles, link targets, escaping, line breaks, the
45	envelope shape) are learned from the capture corpus + the live oracle — none are
46	specified in this file. Byte targets are captured OUTPUT only.

1	//! producers-clean: a clean-room reimplementation of the tamarin-prover
2	//! interactive web UI's fragment PRODUCERS — the code that renders pre-computed
3	//! prover values into the HTML/JSON response-body CONTENT that the already
4	//! clean-roomed dispatch layer serves. Derived purely from black-box
5	//! observation of captured responses + the live oracle. See ../../SPEC.md for
6	//! the observable boundary and the R1–R5 sub-target decomposition, and
7	//! workspace/BEHAVIOR.md for the behavioral spec you build up as you probe.
8	//!
9	//! Status: R1 (center section fragments + the shared HTML skin), R5 (the
10	//! theory-path grammar), R2 (the proof-script west pane assembly), R3 (the
11	//! structured proof-tree lines the pane embeds) and R4 (the welcome/index
12	//! page + housekeeping bodies) IMPLEMENTED — `html` (escape / postprocess /
13	//! envelopes), `section` (`render_pane`, `render_help_pane`), `path`
14	//! (`parse` / `render`), `proofscript` (`render_index`, consuming R5 for
15	//! every link and R3 for every proof display), `prooftree` (`render_tree`),
16	//! and `welcome` (`render_welcome` + housekeeping bodies). Gated by
17	//! tests/round1_center_fragments.rs + tests/corpus_sweep.rs (R1: 324-fragment
18	//! byte parity), tests/r5_path_grammar.rs (R5: live-probe acceptance replay +
19	//! 40037-tail corpus byte round-trip), tests/r2_west_pane.rs (R2+R3: all 478
20	//! overview west panes sliced to opaque formula/method text and re-rendered
21	//! byte-identically + live-probe replays), tests/r3_proof_tree.rs (R3
22	//! fixtures + live autoprove replays), and tests/r4_welcome.rs (R4 live-probe
23	//! byte replays).
24	//!
25	//! In scope: pure-render producers — given pre-computed prover values, emit the
26	//! fragment bytes (tags, links, headings, escaping, line breaks, envelope).
27	//! OUT of scope (opaque input / other clusters): the pretty-printed content
28	//! text itself (formula / rule / signature / method text), the constraint
29	//! system and applicable-proof-methods panes, graph DOT/SVG, and the Rust-only
30	//! progressive-UI route.
31	
32	pub mod model;
33	
34	pub mod html; // R1 — shared skin: escaping, line postprocess, JSON envelope
35	pub mod section; // R1 — theory-view center section fragments (round-1 target)
36	pub mod proofscript; // R2 — proof-script west pane (theory index)
37	pub mod prooftree; // R3 — proof-tree / proof-method HTML
38	pub mod welcome; // R4 — welcome/index page + housekeeping
39	pub mod path; // R5 — theory-path grammar (URL <-> structured)
40	
41	pub use model::*;
42	
43	/// Round-1 entry point: render a theory-view center-section content pane
44	/// (`main/message` / `main/rules` / `main/tactic`) to its response-body bytes.
45	/// Delegates to [`section::render_pane`].
46	pub fn render_content_pane(pane: &model::ContentPane) -> String {
47	    section::render_pane(pane)
48	}
49	
50	/// Round-1 entry point: render the `main/help` pane (env line + static help
51	/// block) to its response-body bytes. Delegates to
52	/// [`section::render_help_pane`].
53	pub fn render_help_pane(help: &model::HelpPane) -> String {
54	    section::render_help_pane(help)
55	}
56	
57	/// Round-2 entry point: parse a raw theory-path wildcard tail (the part after
58	/// `/thy/trace/<idx>/main/`, still percent-encoded) into a structured path.
59	/// Delegates to [`path::parse`].
60	pub fn parse_path(raw: &str) -> Option<model::ThyPath> {
61	    path::parse(raw)
62	}
63	
64	/// Round-2 entry point: render a structured theory path to its href segments
65	/// (percent-encoded; join with `/`). Delegates to [`path::render`].
66	pub fn render_path(p: &model::ThyPath) -> Vec<String> {
67	    path::render(p)
68	}
69	
70	/// Round-2 entry point: render the proof-script WEST pane (the theory index
71	/// shown left of every page) to its inner HTML. Delegates to
72	/// [`proofscript::render_index`].
73	pub fn render_proof_script(pane: &model::ProofScriptPane) -> String {
74	    proofscript::render_index(pane)
75	}
76	
77	/// Round-3 entry point: render a lemma's proof tree as the pre-postprocess
78	/// document fragment the west pane embeds (logical lines joined with
79	/// newlines). Delegates to [`prooftree::render_tree`].
80	pub fn render_proof_tree(index: u64, lemma: &str, tree: &model::ProofTree) -> String {
81	    prooftree::render_tree(index, lemma, tree)
82	}
83	
84	/// Round-3 entry point: render the welcome / index (`/`) page body.
85	/// Delegates to [`welcome::render_welcome`].
86	pub fn render_welcome(w: &model::Welcome) -> String {
87	    welcome::render_welcome(w)
88	}

1 //! Input model — the pre-computed prover VALUES the producers render. 2 //! 3 //! These are the crate's OWN, minimal, behavior-named types for unit testing 4 //! (build a value with Rust constructors, assert its rendered fragment equals an 5 //! observed capture). At integration the open side wires thin adapters from the 6 //! live typed-theory / proof-state values into this shape; see 7 //! ../../interface/ for the integration surface. 8 //! 9 //! Everything here is a value the producer only RENDERS. The text of a formula, 10 //! rule, signature, or proof method is computed elsewhere (the pretty / solver 11 //! side) and reaches the producer as opaque `content` strings — the producer 12 //! adds the HTML/JSON skin (tags, links, escaping, line breaks, envelope) the 13 //! captures show around it. No field here re-derives prover content. 14 15 // --------------------------------------------------------------------------- 16 // R1 — theory-view CENTER section fragments (`main/
`) 17 // --------------------------------------------------------------------------- 18 19 /// A content pane addressed by a `main/
` route, rendered as the 20 /// `{title, html}` response envelope. The pane is an ordered list of headed 21 /// blocks; the HTML skin (heading tags, monospace paragraph, the per-line 22 /// break/indent postprocess) is the producer's to add. 23 pub struct ContentPane { 24 /// Envelope title (e.g. the message / rules / tactic pane's title text). 25 pub title: String, 26 /// Headed blocks, in document order. 27 pub blocks: Vec, 28 } 29 30 /// One headed block: a heading line plus a monospace body. 31 pub struct HeadedBlock { 32 /// Section heading text (a fixed vocabulary chosen per route). 33 pub heading: String, 34 /// Pre-rendered body content (opaque prover text, already line-structured 35 /// and pre-skinned: entity-escaped with any emphasis spans in place). 36 /// Empty is meaningful. 37 pub body: Content, 38 /// How the block renders when its body is empty (BEHAVIOR.md §4). 39 pub when_empty: EmptyRender, 40 } 41 42 /// Empty-body rendering modes, all three observed (BEHAVIOR.md §4). 43 /// 44 /// The interface header models this as `keep_when_empty: bool`; `Keep`/`Omit` 45 /// map onto true/false and `BlankLine` is the rules pane's macros slot, pinned 46 /// by live probe [L03] (macros present ⇒ the pane starts with `

Macros

`, 47 /// absent ⇒ a single blank line where the block would be — the corpus-wide 48 /// leading `
`). The integration adapter picks the mode per section. 49 pub enum EmptyRender { 50 /// Emit heading + an empty paragraph (tactic; message sections by analogy — 51 /// never observed empty). 52 Keep, 53 /// Drop heading + paragraph but leave one blank line in the document (the 54 /// rules pane's macros slot). 55 BlankLine, 56 /// Vanish without residue (the rules pane's restrictions section). 57 Omit, 58 } 59 60 /// The `main/help` pane input (BEHAVIOR.md §8): the env line's fields plus the 61 /// pre-computed wellformedness banner. The static help block is the producer's 62 /// own fixed content. 63 pub struct HelpPane { 64 pub theory_name: String, 65 /// Load wall-clock time as displayed (`HH:MM:SS`). 66 pub load_time: String, 67 /// Load-origin text (e.g. `Local "/tmp/…/thy/file.spthy"`), un-escaped; 68 /// the producer entity-escapes it. 69 pub origin: String, 70 /// Raw pre-rendered `
…
` block, or empty when 71 /// the theory loaded warning-free (opaque load-time input). 72 pub wf_banner_html: String, 73 } 74 75 /// Opaque pre-rendered content: a sequence of logical lines plus, where known, 76 /// which spans are keyword / operator / comment emphasis (used only for the 77 /// byte-close HTML skin; the acceptance gate compares visible text, not skin). 78 pub struct Content { 79 pub lines: Vec, 80 } 81 82 impl Content { 83 pub fn is_empty(&self) -> bool { 84 self.lines.is_empty() || self.lines.iter().all(|l| l.is_empty()) 85 } 86 } 87 88 // --------------------------------------------------------------------------- 89 // R2 — proof-script WEST pane (the theory index shown left of every page) 90 // --------------------------------------------------------------------------- 91 92 /// The whole proof-script pane input. 93 pub struct ProofScriptPane { 94 pub theory_name: String, 95 /// Resolved numeric version index used in every internal link. 96 pub index: u64, 97 /// Top navigation items (message / rules / tactic / sources), in order, 98 /// each with its label and pre-computed annotation. 99 pub items: Vec, 100 pub lemmas: Vec, 101 } 102 103 /// One top-of-index navigation item. 104 /// 105 /// The interface header carries the link target as a `section` string (the 106 /// `main/<…>` wildcard tail); this crate-local model holds it as the R5 107 /// [`ThyPath`] instead so the link is CONSTRUCTED through the theory-path 108 /// grammar — the integration adapter maps the live value via `path::parse`. 109 pub struct NavItem { 110 /// The `main/<…>` route the item links to (rendered via R5). 111 pub target: ThyPath, 112 /// Bold label text (opaque pre-rendered input; the observed vocabulary is 113 /// fixed per slot — BEHAVIOR.md §12). 114 pub label: String, 115 /// Trailing annotation text (a count / cases summary), possibly empty 116 /// (opaque pre-rendered input). 117 pub annotation: String, 118 } 119 120 /// One lemma's index entry: its declaration plus how its proof displays. 121 pub struct LemmaEntry { 122 pub name: String, 123 /// Attribute list text (already assembled by the pretty side), e.g. 124 /// `" [reuse]"`, or empty. May span several logical lines (long heuristic 125 /// lists wrap, with the continuation indent baked into the text). 126 pub attributes: String, 127 /// Trace-quantifier keyword (`all-traces` / `exists-trace`). 128 pub quantifier: String, 129 /// Pre-rendered formula body (opaque prover text, line-structured), at 130 /// indents RELATIVE to the declaration body: the first line carries no 131 /// leading spaces, continuation lines their own deeper indent. The 132 /// renderer adds the 2-space block indent and decides the inline/vertical 133 /// quantifier layout (BEHAVIOR.md §13). 134 pub formula: Content, 135 pub proof: ProofDisplay, 136 } 137 138 /// How a lemma's proof renders in the index. 139 pub enum ProofDisplay { 140 /// Unproven: a single trailing `by sorry` step (no status wrapper). 141 Unproven, 142 /// A structured proof tree, laid out by the R3 renderer 143 /// ([`crate::prooftree`]). The lemma HEADER wrapper span is derived from 144 /// the ROOT node's status (a status-less root — an incomplete proof — 145 /// leaves the header unwrapped) [S16][S19]. 146 Tree(ProofTree), 147 } 148 149 // --------------------------------------------------------------------------- 150 // R3 — proof-tree + proof-method HTML 151 // --------------------------------------------------------------------------- 152 153 /// A proof tree: a method-labelled node with named child cases. Pre-computed; 154 /// the producer only lays it out as nested HTML (indent, keywords, links). 155 pub struct ProofTree { 156 /// Pre-rendered proof-method text for this node (opaque prover value; 157 /// may span several lines, continuation indent baked in). 158 pub method_text: String, 159 /// Highlight status this node's step and structural keywords carry. 160 pub status: Highlight, 161 /// Whether this node is part of the real proof (linked step + remove 162 /// affordance) vs a replayed proof-script leftover rendered without links 163 /// [S20]. 164 pub live: bool, 165 /// A terminal MARKER line (the `SOLVED`-style pre-rendered terminal 166 /// state), which never takes the `by ` prefix a case-less method step 167 /// carries [S19]. 168 pub terminal_marker: bool, 169 /// Named child cases, in order; the empty name means a single unnamed 170 /// continuation (no `case` label, URL segment `_`). 171 pub cases: Vec<(String, ProofTree)>, 172 } 173 174 /// The per-step highlight the observed status classes encode. 175 pub enum Highlight { 176 None, 177 Good, 178 Bad, 179 Medium, 180 Replayed, 181 } 182 183 // --------------------------------------------------------------------------- 184 // R4 — welcome / index page + housekeeping 185 // --------------------------------------------------------------------------- 186 187 /// The index (`/`) page input: the loaded-theory table plus a one-shot banner. 188 pub struct Welcome { 189 pub version: String, 190 pub banner: Banner, 191 pub rows: Vec, 192 } 193 194 pub enum Banner { 195 None, 196 Loaded, 197 Failed, 198 Custom(String), 199 } 200 201 /// One row of the loaded-theory table (all fields supplied verbatim). 202 pub struct TheoryRow { 203 pub index: u64, 204 pub name: String, 205 pub time: String, 206 /// A modified (derived) version renders differently from a primary one. 207 pub modified: bool, 208 pub origin: String, 209 } 210 211 // --------------------------------------------------------------------------- 212 // R5 — theory-path grammar (URL <-> structured path) 213 // --------------------------------------------------------------------------- 214 215 /// A theory-internal path parsed from / rendered to the wildcard URL segment 216 /// after `/thy/trace///`. 217 #[derive(Debug, PartialEq, Eq, Clone)] 218 pub enum ThyPath { 219 Help, 220 Message, 221 Rules, 222 Tactic, 223 Sources { refined: bool, source_idx: usize, case_idx: usize }, 224 Lemma(String), 225 Proof { lemma: String, sub: Vec }, 226 Edit(String), 227 Add(String), 228 Delete(String), 229 } 230

#!/usr/bin/env bash
# Web-parity gate: semantic (structural) equivalence of the interactive web
# server between HS (oracle) and the Rust port, across a corpus of theory
# files.  The web analogue of corpus_file_diff.sh.
#
# Per file, two strictly-sequential phases so HS and RS never contend:
#   Phase 1 (HS): boot `HS tamarin-prover interactive` on a temp workdir with
#                 the one theory, crawl it (web_crawl.py), cache the response
#                 manifest content-keyed by sha256(file) under .web_hs_cache/.
#   Phase 2 (RS): boot RS on the same workdir, crawl, diff (web_diff.py) the
#                 two manifests semantically (web_normalize.py) → per-url rows.
#
# Env: FILE_TIMEOUT (per-file cap, 300s), READY_TIMEOUT (server-boot wait, 90s),
#      HS_PORT (3021), RS_PORT (3022), CORPUS_ROOT (tamarin-prover/examples/), ALLOWLIST
#      (one relpath/line; default = seed list below), RESULTS_TSV, MAX_NODES
#      (400), CACHE, HS_PATH, RS_PATH, MAUDE_PATH, TAM_RS_NO_AUTO_BUILD.
# Output TSV (6 col): file  url  status  hs_http  rs_http  kind
#   status ∈ MATCH | DIFF | MISSING_RS | MISSING_HS | SKIP_*
set -u
script_dir="$(cd "$(dirname "${BASH_SOURCE[0]}")" && pwd)"
repo_root="$(cd "$script_dir/.." && pwd)"

FILE_TIMEOUT="${FILE_TIMEOUT:-300}"
READY_TIMEOUT="${READY_TIMEOUT:-90}"
HS_PORT="${HS_PORT:-3021}"
RS_PORT="${RS_PORT:-3022}"
CORPUS_ROOT="${CORPUS_ROOT:-$repo_root/tamarin-prover/examples}"
CACHE="${CACHE:-$script_dir/.web_hs_cache}"
RESULTS_TSV="${RESULTS_TSV:-/tmp/web_parity.tsv}"
MAX_NODES="${MAX_NODES:-400}"

1	// Currently GPL 3.0 until granted permission by the following authors:

5	// Ported from upstream tamarin-prover sources:

9	//! HTTP server for the Tamarin prover (Rust port) interactive UI.
10	//!
11	//! Goal: serve the existing `frontend/` (TypeScript + d3 + viz-js)
12	//! and the static assets under `data/` (jQuery, CSS, images) without
13	//! modifying any frontend code.  The route shape closely mirrors
14	//! Haskell's `Web.Dispatch` — same URL layout and the same JSON
15	//! response envelope (`{ html, title }` / `{ alert }` / `{ redirect }`)
16	//! — with one Rust-specific addition: `/thy/trace/:idx/proof-step/*path`
17	//! for the progressive UI, which has no counterpart in Haskell's route
18	//! table (`Web/Types.hs`).
19	//!
20	//! Wiring:
21	//!
22	//!   `tamarin-prover interactive theory.spthy`  →
23	//!     - load theory via `tamarin_parser::parse_theory`
24	//!     - elaborate via `tamarin_theory::elaborate::elaborate`
25	//!     - boot Maude (`tamarin_term::maude_proc::MaudeHandle`)
26	//!     - register the theory in a `TheoryStore`
27	//!     - serve the bundled `frontend/dist/` (+ `data/`) on a TCP port
28	//!
29	//! Routes (subset, matching Haskell):
30	//!
31	//!   GET  /                                                  RootR
32	//!   POST /                                                  RootR (file upload)
33	//!   GET  /thy/trace/<idx>/overview/*path                    InteractiveOverviewR
34	//!   GET  /thy/trace/<idx>/main/*path                        TheoryPathMR
35	//!   GET  /thy/trace/<idx>/source                            TheorySourceR
36	//!   GET  /thy/trace/<idx>/autoprove/<ext>/<bound>/<quit>/*p AutoProverR
37	//!   GET  /thy/trace/<idx>/unload                            UnloadTheoryR
38	//!   GET  /static/*                                          StaticR (serve data/ + frontend/dist/)
39	//!   GET  /favicon.ico                                       FaviconR
40	//!   GET  /robots.txt                                        RobotsR
41	//!
42	//! Implemented: most trace-theory routes are wired (see `routes.rs`),
43	//! including `overview`, `main`, `source`, `message`, `autoprove`,
44	//! `autoproveAll`, `verify`, `next`, `prev`, `download`, `reload`,
45	//! `get_and_append`, `proof-step`, `del/path`, `unload`, and graph
46	//! rendering (`intdot`/`graph`/`interactive-graph-def` render live SVG
47	//! via the DOT pipeline, with a DOT-text fallback).
48	//!
49	//! Stubs (return a JSON `{alert}` envelope, HTTP 200):
50	//!   - diff theories (`/thy/equiv/...`)
51	//!   - lemma editing (`edit`)
52

54	// …" / "shutting down…" startup+lifecycle messages to stdout by design
55	// (mirroring HS's `Interactive.hs` ready message).  These are not the batch
56	// `--prove` byte-parity surface, so `println!` is the intended mechanism and

82	#[derive(Debug, Clone)]
83	pub struct ServerConfig {
84	    /// Address to bind, e.g. `127.0.0.1:3001`.
85	    pub bind_addr: SocketAddr,
86	    /// Path to the `data/` directory (CSS, JS, images, fonts).
87	    pub data_dir: PathBuf,
88	    /// Path to the bundled frontend output (`frontend/dist/`), if any.
89	    pub frontend_dist: Option<PathBuf>,
90	    /// Path to the Maude binary.
91	    pub maude_path: String,
92	    /// Proof-search step budget threaded to `prove_lemma` for API
93	    /// compatibility. Currently a no-op: the solver bounds search by
94	    /// ID-DFS depth + wall-clock deadline (HS-faithful), so this value
95	    /// is accepted but ignored.
96	    pub max_steps: usize,
97	    /// `--derivcheck-timeout` for the dynamic message-derivation checks
98	    /// run at theory load (HS interactive default 5s; 0 disables).  Set
99	    /// from the CLI flag by `interactive` setup.
100	    pub derivcheck_timeout: u32,
101	    /// CLI `--stop-on-trace` (None = flag absent).  Merged with each
102	    /// theory's in-file `configuration:` block at `ProofState::new` time
103	    /// per HS `closeTheory` precedence (TheoryLoader.hs:640-666): the CLI
104	    /// value wins; the block is consulted only when this is `None`.
105	    pub stop_on_trace: Option<tamarin_theory::constraint::solver::context::CutStrategy>,
106	}
107	
108	impl ServerConfig {
109	    pub fn new(bind_addr: SocketAddr, data_dir: PathBuf, maude_path: String) -> Self {
110	        Self {
111	            bind_addr,
112	            data_dir,
113	            frontend_dist: None,
114	            maude_path,
115	            max_steps: 500,
116	            derivcheck_timeout: 5,
117	            stop_on_trace: None,
118	        }
119	    }
120	}
121	
122	/// Start the server, blocking until shutdown.
123	///
124	/// Initial theory files (paths) are loaded eagerly.
125	pub async fn serve(
126	    cfg: ServerConfig,
127	    theory_paths: Vec<PathBuf>,
128	) -> Result<(), Box<dyn std::error::Error + Send + Sync>> {
129	    // The web UI renders every HTTP response at HS's web width (100/67),
130	    // not the CLI console width (110/73) — HS `getTheorySourceR` uses
131	    // `render` (HughesPJ default `style`) and every HTML fragment goes
132	    // through `renderHtmlDoc`, both width 100.  Set process-wide before
133	    // any rendering.  (Console-only `renderDoc` at 110 has no HTTP
134	    // analogue here.)
135	    tamarin_theory::pretty_hpj::set_display_width(
136	        tamarin_theory::pretty_hpj::WEB_LINE_LENGTH,
137	        tamarin_theory::pretty_hpj::WEB_RIBBON,
138	    );
139	
140	    // Retain each proof node's constraint `System` after expansion.  The
141	    // `--prove` CLI drops them post-expansion to keep RSS low (the text
142	    // proof never reprints a per-node system), but the interactive UI
143	    // renders the annotated system + applicable proof methods at every
144	    // proof path — HS keeps a `Just System` on every `IncrementalProof`
145	    // node.  Must be set before the first `autoprove` runs a search.
146	    tamarin_theory::constraint::solver::search::set_keep_sys(true);
147	
148	    let store = TheoryStore::default();
149	
150	    // Eager-load every command-line theory.  Per-theory stdout reporting
151	    // mirrors HS `loadTheories` (Web/Dispatch.hs:157-198): a non-empty
152	    // wellformedness report is echoed via `ppInteractive`
153	    // (Dispatch.hs:200-209), and a load failure prints the dashed
154	    // `reportFailure` block (Dispatch.hs:191-198) and skips the theory.
155	    for p in &theory_paths {
156	        match theory_io::load_from_path(p, &cfg.maude_path, cfg.derivcheck_timeout) {
157	            Ok(entry) => {
158	                let name = entry.name.clone();
159	                if !entry.wf_report.is_empty() {
160	                    let dashes = "-".repeat(78);
161	                    let report = tamarin_theory::pretty_theory::render_wf_error_report(
162	                        &entry.wf_report,
163	                    );
164	                    println!(
165	                        "{dashes}\nTheory file '{}'\n{dashes}\n\nWARNING: ignoring the following wellformedness errors\n\n{}\n{dashes}\n",
166	                        p.display(),
167	                        report.trim_end_matches('\n'),
168	                    );
169	                }
170	                let idx = store.insert(entry);
171	                tracing::info!(idx, ?name, path = ?p, "loaded theory");
172	            }
173	            Err(e) => {
174	                tracing::error!(error = %e, path = ?p, "failed to load theory");
175	                let dashes = "-".repeat(78);
176	                println!(
177	                    "{dashes}\nUnable to load theory file `{}'\n{dashes}\n\n{}\n{dashes}\n",
178	                    p.display(),
179	                    e,
180	                );
181	            }
182	        }
183	    }
184	
185	    let state = Arc::new(AppState {
186	        cfg: cfg.clone(),
187	        store,
188	    });
189	
190	    let app = router(state.clone());
191	    let listener = tokio::net::TcpListener::bind(cfg.bind_addr).await?;
192	    // HS ready message (Interactive.hs:68-166, see line 104, printed after all theories
193	    // load, Dispatch.hs:149-209, see line 160) — note the trailing space after "at" and the
194	    // indented URL line.
195	    println!(
196	        "Finished loading theories ... server ready at \n\n    http://{}\n",
197	        cfg.bind_addr,
198	    );
199	    axum::serve(listener, app)
200	        .with_graceful_shutdown(shutdown_signal())
201	        .await?;
202	    Ok(())
203	}
204	
205	async fn shutdown_signal() {
206	    let ctrl_c = async { let _ = tokio::signal::ctrl_c().await; };
207	    #[cfg(unix)]
208	    let term = async {
209	        // Degrade gracefully if the SIGTERM handler can't be installed
210	        // (e.g. resource limits): only ctrl_c drives shutdown, instead
211	        // of panicking at startup.  Mirrors the non-unix branch.
212	        match tokio::signal::unix::signal(
213	            tokio::signal::unix::SignalKind::terminate())
214	        {
215	            Ok(mut s) => { s.recv().await; }
216	            Err(e) => {
217	                tracing::warn!(error = %e, "could not install SIGTERM handler; \
218	                    only ctrl_c will trigger shutdown");
219	                std::future::pending::<()>().await;
220	            }
221	        }
222	    };
223	    #[cfg(not(unix))]
224	    let term = std::future::pending::<()>();
225	    tokio::select! {
226	        _ = ctrl_c => {}
227	        _ = term => {}
228	    }
229	    println!("\ntamarin-prover: shutting down...");
230	}

# Boot a server, wait until it answers on / , run the crawl, then kill it
# (whole process group, to reap maude children).  Args: bin port workdir out.
boot_crawl() {
    local bin="$1" port="$2" wd="$3" out="$4" kind="$5"
    local log="$wd/${kind}_server.log" pid
    # Pin the derivcheck budget like corpus_file_diff.sh does (30s): HS's
    # 5s default expires deterministically on ~12 corpus files even idle,
    # replacing the derivation report with a timeout block RS never emits
    # (48 bogus DIFF rows in the 2026-07-05 sweep).  RS parses the flag on
    # its web path too (hardcoded-5s load is a separate, tracked plumb).
    setsid "$bin" interactive "$wd/thy" --port="$port" \
        --derivcheck-timeout="${DERIVCHECK_TIMEOUT:-30}" >"$log" 2>&1 &
    pid=$!
    # wait for readiness
    local ok="" i
    for ((i=0; i<READY_TIMEOUT; i++)); do
        if curl -sf -o /dev/null "http://127.0.0.1:$port/"; then ok=1; break; fi
        kill -0 "$pid" 2>/dev/null || break
        sleep 1
    done
    if [ -z "$ok" ]; then
        echo "  $kind server not ready ($wd)" >&2
        kill -- -"$pid" 2>/dev/null; wait "$pid" 2>/dev/null
        return 1
    fi
    # shellcheck disable=SC2086  # CRAWL_EXTRA_ARGS must word-split
    timeout "$FILE_TIMEOUT" python3 "$script_dir/web_crawl.py" \
        "http://127.0.0.1:$port" "$out" --max-nodes "$MAX_NODES" ${CRAWL_EXTRA_ARGS:-} 2>>"$log"
    local rc=$?
    kill -- -"$pid" 2>/dev/null; wait "$pid" 2>/dev/null
    return $rc
}
